name: Next.js
what_it_does: A React framework with file-based routing, server-side rendering, static generation, and a built-in API layer that covers the full stack from UI to edge functions.
use_cases: Building full-stack web applications where an agent generates both frontend and backend code in a single repository
Server-rendered marketing sites with dynamic data fetching via React Server Components
REST API backends using Next.js API routes or the new App Router route handlers
Edge-deployed middleware for auth, redirects, and A/B testing via Vercel or Cloudflare
Incremental static regeneration for content-heavy sites that need near-real-time freshness without full rebuilds
not_for: Pure backend services with no UI — Express, Fastify, or Hono are lighter
Mobile app development — React Native is the appropriate React choice
Teams locked into a non-Node runtime (e.g., JVM or .NET) where only the frontend matters
best_when: You want a single TypeScript codebase that handles React UI, API routes, and server logic with minimal configuration and first-class Vercel deployment.
avoid_when: Your app is purely a static site with no dynamic data, or you need a framework that runs outside the Node/Edge ecosystem.

auth.notes: Framework — no built-in auth API. Authentication is implemented within apps using NextAuth.js (Auth.js), Clerk, or custom middleware. App Router middleware enables edge-level auth enforcement.

pricing.paid_tiers: Hosting costs vary: Vercel free tier is generous; Pro starts at $20/month per member
pricing.notes: The framework itself is free. Production hosting on Vercel incurs costs for bandwidth, function invocations, and seat count. Self-hosted deployments on any Node-capable platform are also fully supported.

performance.rate_limits: N/A — framework, not a hosted API

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Build errors are descriptive; runtime errors in RSC can be cryptic. The App Router's dual client/server boundary generates hard-to-debug 'use client'/'use server' misuse errors that confuse LLM code generation.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Framework code generation is deterministic. Idempotency of data mutations is the responsibility of the app's API logic.
known_agent_gotchas: App Router vs Pages Router coexist — agents frequently mix patterns from both, producing broken hybrid files
'use client' and 'use server' directives must be at the top of files; agents often place them incorrectly or omit them
Server Components cannot use hooks or browser APIs — agents trained on older React patterns regularly violate this
next/image requires explicit width and height or a fill prop; agents often emit bare tags that fail Next.js lint rules
Route handlers in App Router (route.ts) have different signatures than Pages API routes (req, res) — agents confuse the two

security_score_components.security_notes: Environment variables are cleanly separated (NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix exposes to client). Server Actions and Route Handlers enforce server-side execution. Large dependency tree (React ecosystem) requires active auditing. Next.js has had historical XSS and SSRF CVEs — stay on current minor versions.
